enrichment.short_description: Checkmarx is an enterprise application security testing platform combining SAST, SCA, DAST, and API security scanning in the unified Checkmarx One cloud platform.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Checkmarx's SAST, SCA, DAST, and API security modules work inside Checkmarx One, plus pricing structure and how it compares for enterprise AppSec.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Checkmarx is an application security testing (AppSec) company founded in 2006 by Maty Siman and Emmanuel Benzaquen. It is headquartered in Ramat Gan, Israel, with a major U.S. presence in Atlanta, Georgia, and additional offices across the UK, France, Portugal, India, and Singapore. The company employs more than 900 people and was acquired by private equity firms Hellman & Friedman and TPG in April 2020 for approximately $1.15 billion; its flagship cloud platform, Checkmarx One, reportedly surpassed $150 million in annual recurring revenue in late 2025. Checkmarx's core product is Checkmarx One, a cloud-based application security platform that consolidates multiple scanning technologies into a single interface: Static Application Security Testing (SAST) analyzes source code for vulnerabilities pre-deployment; Software Composition Analysis (SCA) identifies vulnerable and non-compliant open-source dependencies and generates software bills of materials (SBOMs); Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) tests running applications for exploitable vulnerabilities; API Security scans and inventories API endpoints for exposure risks; and additional modules cover Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) scanning, container security, and supply-chain security. Checkmarx has also introduced AI-oriented capabilities, including tooling aimed at securing AI-generated code and agentic development workflows. The platform integrates with common CI/CD pipelines, source control systems (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps), and ticketing tools, and is positioned for organizations running DevSecOps programs at scale. Checkmarx does not publish standard list pricing. Instead, customers select the specific modules relevant to their attack surface (for example, SAST plus SCA, or a full-platform bundle including DAST and API Security) and receive a custom quote, typically scoped to number of developers, scan volume, and contract length. Buyers who bundle multiple modules or commit to multi-year terms commonly negotiate lower effective per-module pricing than standalone purchases; organizations with 50 or more developers often see the largest discounts. Because pricing is not transparent, prospective customers generally need to engage the Checkmarx sales team to get an accurate cost estimate, which can make budget comparison against competitors more difficult than with vendors that publish tiered pricing. Checkmarx is best suited to mid-size and large enterprises, particularly those in regulated industries or building software supply chains, that need a single consolidated AppSec vendor rather than assembling point solutions from multiple providers. Smaller teams or startups with limited security budgets may find the platform's modular licensing and lack of public self-service pricing to be a barrier compared with lighter-weight, self-serve AppSec tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Checkmarx is an application security testing company founded in 2006 and headquartered in Ramat Gan, Israel, with significant U.S. operations centered in Atlanta. The company was acquired by private equity firms Hellman & Friedman and TPG in 2020 in a deal valued at roughly $1.15 billion, and now employs over 900 people serving enterprise customers worldwide.

Its flagship product, Checkmarx One, is a cloud-based application security platform that unifies static analysis, software composition analysis, dynamic testing, and API security scanning into a single console, aimed at organizations running formal DevSecOps programs across large codebases and complex software supply chains.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Checkmarx One's core modules include SAST for scanning source code before deployment, SCA for identifying vulnerable or non-compliant open-source components and generating SBOMs, DAST for testing live applications, and API Security for discovering and assessing exposed API endpoints.

Additional capabilities cover Infrastructure-as-Code scanning, container security, and supply-chain risk detection, along with newer tooling focused on securing AI-generated code. The platform integrates with major CI/CD systems and source control platforms so security checks can run inside existing developer workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Checkmarx does not publish standard pricing tiers. Customers choose which modules (SAST, SCA, DAST, API Security, etc.) match their needs and receive a custom quote based on factors like number of developers, scan volume, and contract term.

Organizations bundling multiple modules or committing to multi-year agreements typically negotiate better effective pricing than those buying a single module standalone, and larger engineering teams often unlock volume discounts.

features.title: Static Application Security Testing (SAST)
features.description: Scans proprietary source code for security vulnerabilities before code is deployed.

features.title: Software Composition Analysis (SCA)
features.description: Detects vulnerable and non-compliant open-source dependencies and generates software bills of materials.

features.title: Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)
features.description: Tests running web applications for exploitable vulnerabilities from the outside in.

features.title: API Security
features.description: Discovers and inventories API endpoints and flags exposure risks, including undocumented shadow APIs.

features.title: Infrastructure-as-Code scanning
features.description: Analyzes Terraform, CloudFormation, and similar IaC templates for misconfigurations.

features.title: Container security
features.description: Scans container images for vulnerabilities and insecure configurations.

features.title: Unified Checkmarx One platform
features.description: Consolidates results from all scanning modules into a single risk-prioritized dashboard.

features.title: CI/CD and SCM integrations
features.description: Plugs into pipelines and source control tools like GitHub, GitLab, and Azure DevOps to run scans automatically.

pros: Consolidates SAST, SCA, DAST, and API security into one platform rather than multiple point tools
Established, mature vendor with broad programming language and framework coverage
Deep CI/CD and developer-workflow integrations for shifting security left
Backed by significant enterprise support infrastructure and a large customer base
cons: No public pricing; getting an accurate cost requires a sales conversation
Modular licensing across SAST, SCA, DAST, and other add-ons can be complex to budget for
Primarily built for mid-size and large enterprises, less accessible for small teams
Can involve a steeper learning curve and tuning period to reduce false positives

faqs.question: What is Checkmarx used for?
faqs.answer: Checkmarx is used for application security testing, covering static code analysis (SAST), open-source dependency scanning (SCA), dynamic testing (DAST), and API security, typically integrated into CI/CD pipelines.

faqs.question: How much does Checkmarx cost?
faqs.answer: Checkmarx does not publish standard pricing. Cost depends on which modules you license, number of developers, and contract length, and requires a custom quote from Checkmarx sales.

faqs.question: Is Checkmarx a SAST or SCA tool?
faqs.answer: Checkmarx offers both. SAST and SCA were its original core products, and the Checkmarx One platform has since expanded to include DAST, API security, IaC scanning, and container security.

faqs.question: Who owns Checkmarx?
faqs.answer: Checkmarx is privately held, owned by private equity firms Hellman & Friedman and TPG following their 2020 acquisition of the company.

faqs.question: Does Checkmarx integrate with CI/CD pipelines?
faqs.answer: Yes. Checkmarx One integrates with common CI/CD tools and source control platforms such as GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure DevOps to automate scans during the development process.

use_cases.title: Enterprise DevSecOps programs
use_cases.description: Large engineering organizations use Checkmarx One to run automated SAST, SCA, and DAST scans across many repositories from a single console.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise application security and platform engineering teams

use_cases.title: Regulated industry compliance
use_cases.description: Companies in finance, healthcare, and government use Checkmarx to document vulnerability management and software supply-chain controls for audits.
use_cases.audience: Compliance and risk teams in regulated sectors

use_cases.title: Software supply-chain security
use_cases.description: Organizations shipping software to customers use SCA and SBOM generation to track open-source risk across their products.

enrichment.short_description: Checkmk is a full-stack IT infrastructure and application monitoring platform with a free open-source Community edition plus paid Pro, Ultimate, and Cloud tiers.
enrichment.seo_title: Checkmk Pricing & Features: Monitoring Platform Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Checkmk's free Community edition and paid Pro, Ultimate, and Cloud tiers, pricing from €190/month, and its 2,000+ integrations for hybrid IT monitoring.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Checkmk is an IT infrastructure and application monitoring platform originally developed by Mathias Kettner, who founded the company in Munich, Germany on January 19, 2007. The underlying software, then called Check_MK, was first published under the GPL license in April 2009, built on top of the Nagios monitoring core. The company was renamed tribe29 GmbH in April 2019 (when the product itself was rebranded from Check_MK to Checkmk), and renamed again to Checkmk GmbH in April 2023. It remains headquartered in Munich, with additional offices in Atlanta and Minneapolis in the United States, serving over 5,000 commercial customers plus a large open-source community. Checkmk provides unified monitoring and observability across networks, servers, databases, cloud workloads, Kubernetes clusters, applications, and end-user experience, with more than 2,000 pre-built integrations (called 'checks' or plugins) covering common infrastructure and software. The product is offered in four editions. Checkmk Community is free and open source, licensed under the GPL, suitable for monitoring roughly up to 100 hosts, and comes with community-only support. Checkmk Pro is a paid self-hosted edition aimed at data-center-scale infrastructure, starting around €190 per month billed annually, adding advanced analytics, automation, distributed monitoring, and professional support with an 8-hours-by-5-days SLA. Checkmk Ultimate, starting around €275 per month billed annually, extends this with full-stack observability, deeper cloud-native integrations, and advanced security/compliance features, supporting environments that scale to 100,000+ hosts through distributed monitoring, with a 10-hours-by-5-days support SLA (upgradeable to 24x5). Checkmk Cloud is the company's SaaS offering, hosted on AWS in Frankfurt or Northern Virginia, starting around €240 per month billed annually (roughly $2,880 per year for a base tier), offering a 99.5% availability guarantee and capped at 50,000 monitored services. Rather than charging strictly per host, Checkmk's commercial pricing is based on a combination of monitored services (infrastructure), custom metrics (application-level), and synthetic tests. Because the Community edition remains fully functional and free indefinitely, Checkmk is commonly adopted first by system administrators and DevOps teams running smaller environments, who then upgrade to Pro or Ultimate as their infrastructure and support needs grow, or move to the Cloud edition if they prefer a managed SaaS deployment rather than operating the monitoring server themselves. Its open-source lineage on top of Nagios gives it broad compatibility with existing monitoring plugins, while its newer editions add modern observability features like Kubernetes-native monitoring and cloud service integrations aimed at hybrid on-premises and cloud infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Checkmk traces back to 2007, when Mathias Kettner founded the company in Munich, Germany, to develop what was then called Check_MK, a monitoring tool built on the open-source Nagios core and first released under the GPL in 2009. The company was renamed tribe29 in 2019 (alongside the Checkmk product rebrand) and became Checkmk GmbH in 2023, remaining headquartered in Munich with offices in Atlanta and Minneapolis.

Today Checkmk is used by more than 5,000 commercial customers plus a large open-source user base to monitor hybrid IT environments spanning networks, servers, databases, cloud workloads, Kubernetes, applications, and user experience, with over 2,000 pre-built integrations available out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Checkmk automatically discovers services across an environment and provides dashboards, alerting, and notification rules for infrastructure and application monitoring. Higher editions add distributed monitoring for large multi-site environments, advanced automation, and deeper cloud-native and Kubernetes integrations.

The Ultimate and Cloud editions extend the platform with full-stack observability and security/compliance-oriented features, while the free Community edition retains the core monitoring engine and plugin ecosystem for smaller deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Checkmk Community is free and open source under the GPL, suited to environments of roughly up to 100 hosts with community-only support. Paid self-hosted editions start with Pro at around €190/month (billed annually) and Ultimate at around €275/month (billed annually), both scaling to much larger environments with professional support SLAs.

Checkmk Cloud, the SaaS edition hosted on AWS, starts at around €240/month (billed annually) with a 99.5% availability guarantee. Commercial pricing is based on monitored services, custom application metrics, and synthetic tests rather than a strict per-host fee.

features.title: 2,000+ pre-built integrations
features.description: Out-of-the-box monitoring plugins for common servers, network devices, databases, and cloud services.

features.title: Auto-discovery
features.description: Automatically detects hosts and services in an environment to speed up monitoring setup.

features.title: Distributed monitoring
features.description: Scales monitoring across multiple sites and tens of thousands of hosts in higher editions.

features.title: Kubernetes and cloud-native monitoring
features.description: Dedicated integrations for containerized and cloud workloads in the Ultimate and Cloud editions.

features.title: Alerting and notification rules
features.description: Configurable rule-based alerting to route incidents to the right teams.

features.title: Full-stack observability
features.description: Combines infrastructure, application, and synthetic monitoring in the higher-tier editions.

pros: Free, fully functional open-source Community edition with no artificial feature caps for small setups
Broad integration library covering networks, servers, cloud, and applications out of the box
Scales from small self-hosted deployments up to distributed monitoring of 100,000+ hosts
Choice between self-hosted (Pro/Ultimate) and managed SaaS (Cloud) deployment models
cons: Commercial pricing (services, metrics, synthetic tests) can be harder to estimate than flat per-host pricing
Community edition support is limited to community channels only
Nagios-based architecture may feel dated compared to newer cloud-native observability tools
Cloud edition caps at 50,000 services, which may not suit the very largest environments

faqs.question: Is Checkmk free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Checkmk Community edition is free and open source under the GPL, suitable for environments of roughly up to 100 hosts, though it includes community-only support.

faqs.question: How much does Checkmk Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Checkmk Pro starts at around €190 per month when billed annually, scaling with the number of monitored services.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Checkmk Pro and Ultimate?
faqs.answer: Ultimate adds full-stack observability, deeper cloud-native and Kubernetes integrations, and advanced security/compliance features on top of everything in Pro, along with a higher support SLA.

faqs.question: Does Checkmk offer a SaaS version?
faqs.answer: Yes, Checkmk Cloud is a managed SaaS edition hosted on AWS, starting at around €240 per month billed annually with a 99.5% availability guarantee.

faqs.question: Is Checkmk based on Nagios?
faqs.answer: The Checkmk Community edition's core still uses the Nagios monitoring engine, extended with Checkmk's own agent, checks, and web interface.

use_cases.title: Data center infrastructure monitoring
use_cases.description: IT teams monitor servers, network devices, and storage across on-premises data centers using Checkmk Pro or Ultimate.
use_cases.audience: System administrators and IT operations teams

use_cases.title: Hybrid and cloud-native observability
use_cases.description: DevOps teams monitor Kubernetes clusters and cloud workloads alongside traditional infrastructure using Checkmk Ultimate or Cloud.

use_cases.title: Small business self-hosted monitoring
use_cases.description: Smaller IT teams deploy the free Community edition to monitor up to roughly 100 hosts without licensing costs.

enrichment.short_description: Checkout.com is a global payment processing platform providing payment gateway, acquiring, card issuing, and fraud prevention infrastructure for enterprise merchants.
enrichment.seo_title: Checkout.com Review: Payments Platform, Fees & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how Checkout.com's payment gateway, card acquiring, and issuing platform works, its interchange++ pricing model, fees, and who it's built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Checkout.com is a financial technology company that provides payment processing infrastructure for enterprise merchants. It was originally founded in 2009 as Opus Payments by Guillaume Pousaz, a Swiss entrepreneur, and rebranded to Checkout.com in 2012. The company is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, and by the mid-2020s employed roughly 2,000 people across around 19 global offices. Checkout.com became a principal member of Visa and Mastercard by 2013 and later secured direct membership status with UnionPay, Diners, Discover, American Express, and JCB, allowing it to act as both a payment gateway and an acquirer in many markets rather than relying entirely on third-party banking partners. The company has raised roughly $3.8 billion in total funding across multiple rounds, including a $230 million Series A in 2019 and a $1 billion Series D in January 2022 that valued the company at $40 billion, making it briefly Europe's most valuable fintech startup at the time. Following broader fintech valuation corrections, the company's valuation was later marked down to approximately $12 billion by 2025, around the same time the company reported reaching profitability in 2024. Checkout.com has also made several acquisitions to expand its capabilities, including Pin Payments (2020), ProcessOut, Icefire (2021), and identity-verification company Ubble (2022). Its product suite includes Flow, a customizable prebuilt payment interface for websites; Issuing, a platform for creating physical and virtual payment cards; IDV, an AI-powered video identity verification tool with facial recognition; Fraud Detection Pro, a machine-learning-based transaction fraud prevention system; Intelligent Acceptance, an AI system designed to optimize payment authorization/approval rates; Business Account, a multi-currency fund management tool for merchants; and Payouts, for sending funds to recipients globally. Checkout.com uses an interchange++ pricing model, which separates the underlying interchange fee, card network scheme fee, and the company's own markup so merchants can see each cost component rather than a single blended rate. Published flat-rate pricing for standard merchants is 0.95% plus $0.20 per transaction for European cards and 2.90% plus $0.20 per transaction for non-European cards; qualifying Enterprise customers with higher volume can negotiate down to interchange plus a markup as low as roughly 0.10%-0.40% plus $0.08 per transaction. Because exact enterprise pricing depends on transaction volume, business model, and geographic footprint, merchants generally need to contact Checkout.com's sales team for a tailored quote. The platform is aimed primarily at mid-size to large global enterprises, marketplaces, and platforms that need a single API to accept payments, issue cards, and manage risk across multiple regions and currencies, rather than smaller merchants who may be better served by simpler, flat-fee payment providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Checkout.com was founded in 2009 as Opus Payments by Guillaume Pousaz and rebranded to Checkout.com in 2012. Headquartered in London, the company grew into a global payments infrastructure provider with direct membership status across major card networks including Visa, Mastercard, American Express, UnionPay, and JCB, letting it operate as both a payment gateway and acquirer in many regions.

The company has raised approximately $3.8 billion, peaked at a $40 billion valuation in 2022, and reported reaching profitability in 2024, with a later valuation of roughly $12 billion reflecting broader fintech market corrections. It employs around 2,000 people across roughly 19 offices worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Checkout.com's core product, Flow, provides a customizable prebuilt payment interface, while Issuing lets businesses create physical and virtual cards. IDV offers AI-powered video identity verification, and Fraud Detection Pro applies machine learning to catch fraudulent transactions.

Intelligent Acceptance uses AI to optimize payment authorization rates, Business Account provides multi-currency fund management, and Payouts enables global disbursements — together forming a single API-driven platform for accepting, managing, and moving money.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Checkout.com uses an interchange++ pricing model that separates interchange, network scheme fees, and its own markup for transparency. Published flat rates are 0.95% plus $0.20 per transaction for European cards and 2.90% plus $0.20 for non-European cards.

Enterprise merchants with sufficient volume can negotiate custom interchange-plus pricing, sometimes as low as interchange plus 0.10%-0.40% and $0.08 per transaction, but exact enterprise rates require contacting the Checkout.com sales team.

features.description: A customizable, prebuilt checkout UI merchants can embed on their websites.

features.description: Create and manage physical and virtual payment cards through the Issuing platform.

features.title: Fraud Detection Pro
features.description: Machine-learning-based fraud prevention applied to transactions in real time.

features.title: Intelligent Acceptance
features.description: AI system that optimizes transaction approval rates to reduce false declines.

features.title: Multi-currency Business Account
features.description: Hold and manage funds across multiple currencies from a single account.

features.title: Global payouts
features.description: Send funds to recipients internationally through a unified payouts API.

pros: Direct card network membership across Visa, Mastercard, and other networks in many markets
Interchange++ pricing offers more fee transparency than blended-rate processors
Single API covering payments, issuing, identity verification, and fraud prevention
Built for global, multi-currency, high-volume enterprise operations
cons: Enterprise pricing is not fully public and requires a sales conversation for an accurate quote
Primarily designed for larger merchants; may be more than smaller businesses need
Integration and onboarding can be more involved than plug-and-play payment providers
Standard flat-rate pricing for non-European cards is higher than some competitors

faqs.question: What is Checkout.com?
faqs.answer: Checkout.com is a global payment processing company offering a payment gateway, card acquiring, card issuing, identity verification, and fraud prevention through a single API.

faqs.question: How much does Checkout.com charge per transaction?
faqs.answer: Published flat rates are 0.95% plus $0.20 for European cards and 2.90% plus $0.20 for non-European cards; larger merchants can negotiate custom interchange++ rates.

faqs.question: Where is Checkout.com headquartered?
faqs.answer: Checkout.com is headquartered in London, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Is Checkout.com a bank?
faqs.answer: No, Checkout.com is a payment services provider and acquirer, not a licensed bank, though it holds direct membership with major card networks.

faqs.question: What is interchange++ pricing?
faqs.answer: Interchange++ separates a transaction's cost into the underlying interchange fee, the card network's scheme fee, and the processor's own markup, giving merchants visibility into each component.

use_cases.title: Global enterprise ecommerce
use_cases.description: Large online retailers use Checkout.com to accept payments across multiple currencies and card networks worldwide.

use_cases.title: Marketplaces and platforms
use_cases.description: Multi-sided marketplaces use Checkout.com's payments and payouts APIs to collect from buyers and disburse to sellers.

use_cases.title: Fraud and risk management
use_cases.description: Merchants use Fraud Detection Pro and Intelligent Acceptance to reduce chargebacks while maximizing approval rates.

enrichment.short_description: Chevereto is self-hosted image and video hosting software for building a private, fully customizable media-sharing website similar to Imgur or Flickr.
enrichment.seo_title: Chevereto Review: Self-Hosted Image Hosting Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chevereto lets you self-host your own image and video hosting site. See its free open-source edition, paid Lite/Pro licenses, and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chevereto is self-hosted image and video hosting software created by Rodolfo Berríos, a software engineer based in Concepción, Chile. The project has been in development since 2007, making it one of the longer-running self-hosted media platforms, and was formally established as a company in 2012. Chevereto lets individuals, communities, and businesses run their own private image and video sharing website, comparable in concept to Imgur or Flickr, but fully under the operator's control since it runs on the operator's own server rather than a third-party cloud service. The software is a PHP application (with JavaScript and CSS for the frontend) that requires a web server, a MySQL or MariaDB database, and optionally Redis for caching, and it can be deployed via Docker, on a VPS, or through control panels like cPanel and Plesk. Chevereto is distributed under two licensing models. The Free edition is open source under the AGPLv3 license and available on GitHub, providing core image/video hosting functionality at no cost. Chevereto Lite and Chevereto Pro are distributed under a proprietary commercial license: buyers purchase a subscription that grants a license key with access to updates, bug fixes, and support for a limited period, but any version released during an active subscription can continue to be used perpetually afterward, even if the subscription lapses. Key features across editions include multi-user accounts with registration, user profiles, two-factor authentication, and role-based access control; albums, tags, and categories for organizing media; multi-tenant capability so a single installation can host multiple independent branded sites; granular privacy controls for content visibility; performance features including CDN support and integration with object storage services like Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, and Alibaba Cloud; support for over 30 languages; and an extensive API for programmatic uploads and integrations. Because Chevereto is self-hosted, adopters are responsible for their own server infrastructure, security patching, and backups, which gives full data ownership but requires more technical involvement than a managed SaaS image host. It's commonly used by web agencies building white-label media-sharing platforms for clients, community and forum operators who want their own private image host instead of relying on third-party services, and individuals or small teams who want full control over uploaded content and its long-term availability rather than depending on a hosting service that could change its terms or shut down.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chevereto is self-hosted image and video hosting software created by Rodolfo Berríos, in development since 2007 and formalized as a company in 2012. It lets operators run their own private, fully branded media-sharing website on their own servers, similar in concept to Imgur or Flickr but without depending on a third-party host.

The software is a PHP application that can be deployed via Docker, a VPS, or standard hosting control panels, and is available under both an open-source AGPLv3 license (Free edition) and a proprietary commercial license (Lite and Pro editions) for teams that want additional features and support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Chevereto supports multi-user accounts with two-factor authentication and role-based access, media organization through albums and tags, and multi-tenant hosting so one installation can serve multiple independent sites. It integrates with CDNs and object storage providers like Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, and Alibaba Cloud.
The platform supports over 30 languages and exposes an extensive API for programmatic uploads, making it suitable for both direct end-user hosting sites and integrations with other applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Chevereto Free edition is open source (AGPLv3) and free to self-host indefinitely. Chevereto Lite and Pro are paid, proprietary-licensed editions sold as subscriptions; a license key grants updates and support during the active subscription period, and any version released during that time can be used perpetually afterward.

Exact Lite and Pro pricing is published on chevereto.com and varies by plan; prospective buyers should check the current rates directly, since Chevereto periodically adjusts pricing for new subscribers while honoring existing subscribers' locked-in rates.

features.title: Multi-user accounts with 2FA
features.description: User registration, profiles, and two-factor authentication with role-based access control.

features.title: Albums and tagging
features.description: Organize uploaded images and videos into albums, categories, and tags.

features.description: Run multiple independent branded media sites from a single Chevereto installation.

features.title: Object storage and CDN integration
features.description: Connect to Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, Alibaba Cloud, and CDNs for scalable media delivery.

features.title: Granular privacy controls
features.description: Set visibility rules for individual images, albums, or entire user accounts.

features.title: 30+ language support
features.description: Built-in internationalization for a broad range of interface languages.

features.title: Extensive API
features.description: Programmatic upload and management API for custom integrations and automation.

pros: Full data ownership through self-hosting instead of relying on a third-party image host
Mature codebase with active development dating back to 2007
Flexible storage options including CDN and major cloud object storage providers
Free open-source edition available alongside paid Lite/Pro tiers for more features
cons: Paid features and priority support are gated behind Lite/Pro commercial licenses
Requires server management skills and ongoing maintenance since it's self-hosted
License subscription needed to keep receiving updates beyond the version already installed
Not a managed SaaS option, so uptime and scaling are the operator's responsibility

faqs.question: Is Chevereto free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chevereto has a free, open-source edition licensed under AGPLv3. Paid Lite and Pro editions with additional features are available under a separate commercial license.

faqs.question: Can Chevereto be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chevereto is designed to be self-hosted on your own server, VPS, or hosting control panel, and can be deployed with Docker.

faqs.question: What database does Chevereto require?
faqs.answer: Chevereto requires MySQL or MariaDB, and optionally supports Redis for caching.

faqs.question: Does Chevereto support cloud storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chevereto integrates with Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, Alibaba Cloud, and CDN services for storing and delivering media.

faqs.question: Who created Chevereto?
faqs.answer: Chevereto was created by Rodolfo Berríos, a software engineer based in Concepción, Chile, with development dating back to 2007.

use_cases.title: White-label media platforms

use_cases.title: Personal or organizational media archive

use_cases.audience: Individuals and small businesses wanting full data ownership

features: Full feature set including multi-tenant hosting

enrichment.short_description: Chibisafe is a free, open-source, self-hosted file and image uploader written in TypeScript that gives you a private, ShareX-compatible vault with shareable links.
enrichment.seo_title: Chibisafe Review: Self-Hosted File Uploader Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chibisafe is a free, open-source, self-hosted file vault with S3 support, ShareX integration, and an API. See its features and setup requirements.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chibisafe is a free, open-source, self-hosted file and image uploader written in TypeScript, maintained by a developer known as Pitu. It is the modern continuation of an earlier project called lolisafe; version 4.0.0 and later were rewritten and rebranded as Chibisafe, with lolisafe representing the earlier 3.x lineage of the same codebase. The project traces back to around 2017 and has accumulated a few thousand stars on GitHub, with an active community of self-hosters. Chibisafe accepts file uploads of essentially any type and returns a shareable link, functioning as a private alternative to public file-sharing or image-hosting services. It supports three access modes: public mode (anyone can upload), user accounts mode (registered users upload to their own space), and invite-only mode (uploads restricted to invited users). Large files are automatically chunked during upload to reduce the chance of network failures interrupting a transfer, and users can generate API keys to interact with the service programmatically, which enables integration with tools like ShareX, a popular Windows screenshot and file-upload utility, as well as a dedicated browser extension for uploading content directly from web pages and an iOS shortcut for uploading from Apple devices. The platform ships with an administrative dashboard that lets operators configure most instance settings directly from the web UI, including the instance name, rate limits, maximum file size, accepted file extensions, and meta descriptions, without needing to edit configuration files by hand. Additional features include S3-compatible object storage support for scalable file storage, a masonry-style gallery for browsing uploaded media, albums and folders with dedicated shareable links, snippet/gist creation for sharing text content, file tagging, per-user storage quotas, and a built-in URL shortener. Chibisafe is released under the MIT license and is typically deployed via Docker, though it can also run directly with Node.js; self-hosting recommendations include a recent Linux distribution (Debian is commonly recommended) with Node.js 12.18.2 or later and build-essential tools installed. As with most self-hosted open-source projects, there is no official managed hosting or paid support tier; users are responsible for their own server, updates, and backups, and support comes primarily through the GitHub repository's issues and community channels rather than a commercial SLA. It is best suited to individuals, Discord/community server admins, and developers who want a private, ShareX-compatible file host they fully control, rather than relying on third-party public file-sharing services with unclear data retention policies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chibisafe is a free, open-source, self-hosted file and image uploader written in TypeScript, maintained by developer Pitu. It is the TypeScript rewrite and rebrand of the earlier lolisafe project, with Chibisafe representing version 4.0.0 and later of that same codebase, dating back to around 2017.

The service accepts virtually any file type and returns a shareable link, giving operators a private alternative to public file-sharing services, with support for public, registered-user, and invite-only access modes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Chibisafe includes automatic chunking for large file uploads, API keys for programmatic access, and out-of-the-box ShareX support alongside a dedicated browser extension and iOS shortcut. Its admin dashboard lets operators configure rate limits, file size caps, allowed extensions, and other instance settings entirely from the web UI.
Additional features include S3-compatible storage support, a masonry gallery view, albums and folders, snippet/gist sharing, tagging, per-user quotas, and a built-in URL shortener.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Chibisafe is completely free and released under the MIT license. There is no paid tier or official managed hosting offering; users self-host the software on their own server or VPS, typically via Docker.
Because there's no commercial vendor behind it, support is community-driven through the project's GitHub repository rather than a paid support contract.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT license
Full data ownership through self-hosting rather than a third-party uploader
Native ShareX, browser extension, and iOS shortcut support for fast uploads
Actively maintained with a real GitHub community and Docker-based deployment
cons: Requires your own server or VPS and ongoing maintenance to self-host
No official managed hosting or paid enterprise support option
Feature set has changed across major versions (lolisafe to Chibisafe), requiring migration care on upgrades
Support is community-based via GitHub rather than a guaranteed SLA

faqs.question: Is Chibisafe free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chibisafe is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Is Chibisafe related to lolisafe?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chibisafe is the TypeScript rewrite and rebrand of the lolisafe project, starting with version 4.0.0, maintained by the same developer, Pitu.

faqs.question: Does Chibisafe support ShareX?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chibisafe supports ShareX out of the box, making it easy to upload screenshots and files directly from Windows.

faqs.question: Can Chibisafe use S3 storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chibisafe supports S3-compatible object storage as a backend for uploaded files.

faqs.question: How do I deploy Chibisafe?
faqs.answer: Chibisafe is most commonly deployed via Docker, though it can also run directly with Node.js on a Linux server such as Debian.

enrichment.short_description: ChiefOnboarding is an open-source employee onboarding platform where new hires complete preboarding tasks, sequences, and training through Slack or a web portal.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ChiefOnboarding is a free, self-hosted, open-source employee onboarding platform with deep Slack integration. Compare its pricing plans and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ChiefOnboarding is a free and open-source employee onboarding platform founded by Stan Triepels in 2016. It lets new hires complete their onboarding process entirely through Slack or through a standalone web portal, with both interfaces offering full feature parity so companies can choose whichever fits their existing workflow. The software is licensed under AGPLv3 and can be self-hosted on a company's own infrastructure or run through the company's managed hosting option, with no lock-in between the two deployment models. Core functionality includes preboarding pages, where employers build informational pages with text, images, files, and checklists to welcome new hires before their official start date; sequences and drip-fed tasks, which let HR teams schedule to-do items to release over time or trigger automatically based on completion of a prior task; automated account provisioning, which integrates with third-party tools such as Slack, Google Workspace, Asana, and GitHub to automatically create user accounts for new employees as part of the onboarding flow; a knowledge base and courses module, where new hires can search a resource library and work through structured courses at their own pace; and badges and gamification features designed to reward task completion and sustain engagement during the first weeks on the job. The platform supports eight interface languages: English, Dutch, Portuguese, German, Turkish, French, Spanish, and Japanese, making it usable for onboarding programs spanning multiple regions. ChiefOnboarding's pricing follows a simple two-tier model. The Free plan costs €0 per month and covers self-hosting the software on your own infrastructure, with support limited to community channels via GitHub issues; this plan is aimed at organizations with an internal IT team capable of managing their own deployment without vendor assistance. The Support plan costs €249 per month, billed annually and excluding VAT, and adds priority email support with a response time generally under two hours, while still being self-hosted — this plan targets organizations that want to retain full control of their onboarding data on their own servers but also want responsive vendor assistance when issues arise. EU-based businesses purchasing the Support plan must provide a valid VAT ID. Because it's open source, companies with development resources can also inspect, modify, or extend the codebase directly. ChiefOnboarding is best suited to remote-first and distributed companies that already run their internal communication through Slack and want onboarding tightly integrated into that workflow, as well as organizations that prioritize owning and controlling their onboarding and HR data on self-managed infrastructure rather than depending on a third-party SaaS HR platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ChiefOnboarding is a free and open-source employee onboarding platform founded by Stan Triepels in 2016. New hires can complete onboarding entirely through Slack or a standalone web portal, both with full feature access, and the software can be self-hosted or deployed via the project's managed hosting option.

Licensed under AGPLv3, ChiefOnboarding gives HR and IT teams full control over onboarding data and workflows, positioning it as an alternative to closed SaaS onboarding platforms for organizations that want to self-host or customize the codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Employers build preboarding pages to welcome new hires before their start date, and use sequences to drip-feed onboarding tasks over time or trigger them based on prior task completion. Automated account provisioning integrates with tools like Slack, Google Workspace, Asana, and GitHub to create new-hire accounts automatically.

A built-in knowledge base and course system helps new hires ramp up at their own pace, and badges/gamification features are included to keep engagement high during the first weeks. The interface is available in eight languages, supporting multi-region onboarding programs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ChiefOnboarding offers a Free plan at €0/month for self-hosting with community support through GitHub issues, aimed at teams with their own IT capacity. The Support plan costs €249/month, billed annually and excluding VAT, and adds priority email support with typical response times under two hours while remaining self-hosted.

Both plans keep data on the customer's own infrastructure; ChiefOnboarding also offers a managed hosting option outside the self-hosted pricing above for organizations that don't want to run their own servers.

cons: Free plan support is limited to community GitHub issues, with no guaranteed response time
Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance
Smaller ecosystem and fewer third-party integrations than large HR SaaS suites
Priority support requires the paid €249/month plan

faqs.question: Is ChiefOnboarding free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ChiefOnboarding has a free, self-hosted plan with community support. A paid Support plan at €249/month adds priority email support.

faqs.question: Does ChiefOnboarding work with Slack?
faqs.answer: Yes, new hires can complete the entire onboarding process through Slack, with full feature parity to the web portal.

faqs.question: Is ChiefOnboarding open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, ChiefOnboarding is open source under the AGPLv3 license, and the code is available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can ChiefOnboarding automate account creation?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can automatically provision accounts in tools like Slack, Google Workspace, Asana, and GitHub as part of the onboarding sequence.

faqs.question: What languages does ChiefOnboarding support?
faqs.answer: ChiefOnboarding's interface supports English, Dutch, Portuguese, German, Turkish, French, Spanish, and Japanese.

enrichment.short_description: Chitchatter is a free, open-source, serverless peer-to-peer chat app offering encrypted messaging, file transfer, and video calls with nothing stored on a server.
enrichment.seo_title: Chitchatter Review: Serverless P2P Encrypted Chat
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chitchatter is a free, open-source, peer-to-peer chat app with no servers, no accounts, and no message history. See how it works and its features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chitchatter is a free, open-source, peer-to-peer communication application created by developer Jeremy Kahn and available at chitchatter.im, with source code hosted on GitHub under the jeremyckahn/chitchatter repository. It is licensed under GPL v2. The application is described by its own maintainers as serverless, decentralized, and ephemeral: it runs entirely as a client-side web app with no dedicated Chitchatter backend server, and no user accounts or sign-up process are required to use it. To establish connections between users, Chitchatter relies on WebRTC for direct peer-to-peer data channels, using public WebTorrent trackers to help peers discover each other, and falling back to STUN/TURN relay servers only when a direct peer-to-peer connection cannot be established due to restrictive network conditions such as symmetric NATs or firewalls. Because there is no central Chitchatter server storing data, the application has no analytics, tracking, or telemetry of any kind built in. Messages exchanged in a chat room are never persisted to disk on either party's device; when a user leaves a room, that room's message history is cleared from memory and cannot be recovered by either party or by a third party, since it was never stored anywhere in the first place. Room names are, by default, random UUIDs generated entirely on the client side, and users share a room's URL out-of-band (for example, over a separate secure channel) with anyone they want to invite. Rooms can be public, where anyone who has or guesses the room URL can join, or effectively private, where access is limited to those who receive the specific room link. Within a room, Chitchatter supports text messaging, audio and video calling, and screen sharing between multiple peers simultaneously, and it supports file transfers of unlimited size, with files encrypted client-side before being sent over the peer-to-peer connection. Because Chitchatter is a static, client-rendered web application with no required backend infrastructure beyond publicly available WebRTC signaling and relay servers, it can also be self-hosted by anyone who wants to run their own instance rather than relying on the public chitchatter.im deployment, which is useful for organizations or individuals wanting additional assurance about which signaling infrastructure is being used. Chitchatter is best suited to privacy-conscious individuals who want a throwaway, no-signup chat room for a single conversation or file transfer, to journalists, researchers, or activists who need ephemeral communications that leave no server-side record, and to developers interested in a lightweight, self-hostable, open-source example of a fully client-side WebRTC chat application. It is not designed to replace persistent team chat tools, since by design it retains no message history and offers no built-in user directory, workspace, or admin management features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Chitchatter is a free, open-source, peer-to-peer chat application created by developer Jeremy Kahn and hosted publicly at chitchatter.im, with source available on GitHub under the GPL v2 license. It is described as serverless, decentralized, and ephemeral: there is no dedicated Chitchatter backend server, no accounts, and no persisted message history.

Peer connections are established via WebRTC, using public WebTorrent trackers for peer discovery and STUN/TURN relay servers only as a fallback when a direct connection isn't possible, keeping the architecture lightweight and largely serverless.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Within a chat room, Chitchatter supports text messaging, audio and video calls, and screen sharing between multiple peers. File transfers of unlimited size are supported, with files encrypted client-side before being sent over the peer-to-peer connection.

Rooms use random, client-generated UUIDs as names by default and can be shared privately via URL or left open as public rooms. Since nothing is stored server-side, there is no analytics, tracking, or telemetry, and messages are cleared from memory the moment a user leaves a room.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Chitchatter is completely free to use at chitchatter.im, with no accounts, subscriptions, or paid tiers. Because it's open source and largely serverless, anyone can also self-host their own instance of the client-side application at no licensing cost.
There is no commercial entity monetizing Chitchatter, so all functionality, including video calls and unlimited-size file transfer, is available without payment.

features.description: Supports file transfers of unlimited size, encrypted client-side before sending.

features.title: Video, audio, and screen sharing

features.title: Public and private rooms
features.description: Rooms use random UUIDs by default and can be shared privately or left open publicly.

features.title: No tracking or telemetry
features.description: No analytics, tracking, or telemetry of any kind is built into the application.

pros: Strong privacy design: no server-side storage, no accounts, no tracking
Completely free and open source under GPL v2
No sign-up friction; join a room and start chatting immediately
Supports rich media including video calls, screen sharing, and unlimited file size transfers
cons: Peer-to-peer connection reliability depends on network conditions and NAT/firewall traversal
No message history or persistence by design, so nothing can be recovered after leaving a room
No built-in user directory, workspace, or admin/moderation controls
Not intended as a replacement for persistent team chat or enterprise messaging tools

faqs.question: Is Chitchatter free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chitchatter is completely free, open source, and requires no account or subscription to use.

faqs.question: Does Chitchatter store my messages?
faqs.answer: No, messages are never persisted to disk and are cleared from memory as soon as you leave a chat room.

faqs.question: Does Chitchatter require a server?
faqs.answer: Chitchatter has no dedicated backend chat server; it relies on WebRTC peer-to-peer connections, using public WebTorrent trackers for discovery and STUN/TURN servers only as a fallback.

faqs.question: Can Chitchatter be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, since Chitchatter is a client-side, largely serverless open-source application, anyone can host their own instance of it.

faqs.question: Does Chitchatter support video calls?
faqs.answer: Yes, Chitchatter supports audio and video calling as well as screen sharing between multiple peers in a room.

enrichment.short_description: Choco is a B2B growth and ordering platform connecting restaurants with food and beverage distributors, offering AI-assisted order processing and sales tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Choco Review: B2B Food Ordering & Distributor Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Choco connects restaurants and food distributors with AI-powered ordering, catalogs, and a sales rep app. Restaurants use it free; suppliers pay custom rates.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Choco is a business-to-business technology platform for the food and beverage supply chain, founded in 2018 in Berlin, Germany by Daniel Khachab, Rogerio Da Silva Yokomizo, and Julian Hammer. The company connects restaurants with the food and beverage wholesalers and distributors that supply them, aiming to digitize an ordering process that has historically relied heavily on phone calls, text messages, and handwritten notes. Choco has raised over $300 million in funding from investors including Bessemer Venture Partners, Coatue, Insight Partners, G Squared, and Left Lane Capital, reaching unicorn status with a valuation of roughly $1.2 billion. On the restaurant side, Choco provides a free mobile and web app (available on iOS and Android) that lets kitchen staff and managers place orders with multiple suppliers from one place, track order status, and reduce ordering errors compared to phone- or text-based ordering. On the supplier and distributor side, Choco positions itself as a 'growth platform,' offering tools including a real-time digital product catalog that reps and customers can reference while ordering, AI-powered order processing that lets sales representatives log orders quickly with reduced manual data entry, smart AI-generated alerts that flag upsell opportunities or signs of customer churn risk based on ordering pattern changes, and prospecting tools that let sales reps scan a restaurant's menu to infer likely product needs and suggest relevant items when pursuing new accounts. Choco also offers a dedicated Sales Rep mobile app, introduced to give field sales representatives at distributors a way to manage accounts, view catalogs, and log orders from a phone while visiting customers. As of the mid-2020s, Choco's platform focused on order and relationship management rather than payment processing; the company did not offer built-in invoicing or payment processing as part of its core feature set. Pricing follows a two-sided model: the restaurant-facing ordering app is free for restaurants to use, with no listed fees. Suppliers and distributors pay for access to Choco's growth platform under a custom pricing model rather than published flat-rate plans; the exact fee depends on factors such as deployment type, number of users, and which additional features (like the AI-powered sales rep tools) are included, with third-party estimates suggesting supplier costs can range roughly from $20 to $25 per customer account per month, though this is not an officially published rate. Prospective supplier customers need to contact Choco's sales team for a specific quote. Choco is best suited to independent restaurants and restaurant groups looking to simplify multi-supplier ordering at no cost, and to food and beverage distributors and wholesalers seeking to modernize their sales operations, reduce manual order-taking overhead, and use AI-driven insights to grow existing customer accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Choco is a B2B technology platform for the food and beverage supply chain, founded in 2018 in Berlin, Germany by Daniel Khachab, Rogerio Da Silva Yokomizo, and Julian Hammer. It connects restaurants with the distributors that supply them, replacing phone- and text-based ordering with a digital ordering and catalog platform.

The company has raised over $300 million in venture funding and reached unicorn status at a roughly $1.2 billion valuation, with backers including Bessemer Venture Partners, Coatue, and Insight Partners.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Restaurants use Choco's free mobile and web app to place and track orders with multiple suppliers from a single place, reducing errors compared to phone or text ordering. Distributors and their sales teams get a real-time digital catalog, AI-assisted order logging, and smart alerts that flag upsell opportunities or churn risk based on changes in ordering patterns.

A dedicated Sales Rep app lets field reps manage accounts, browse catalogs, and log orders from their phone while visiting customer restaurants, and prospecting tools let reps scan a restaurant's menu to suggest relevant products when courting new accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Choco's restaurant-facing ordering app is free to use, with no published fees for restaurants. Suppliers and distributors pay for the platform under a custom pricing model based on usage, number of users, and selected features, rather than fixed published tiers.

Third-party estimates suggest supplier pricing can fall roughly in the $20-$25 per customer account per month range, but this is not an officially published rate, and suppliers need to contact Choco's sales team for an exact quote.

features.description: Flags upsell opportunities or customer churn risk based on changes in ordering behavior.

pros: Free for restaurants, removing cost as a barrier to adopting digital ordering
AI-assisted order logging and alerts help distributor sales teams work more efficiently
Consolidates multi-supplier ordering into a single app for restaurant staff
Backed by significant funding and unicorn status, suggesting platform stability and continued investment
cons: Supplier pricing is not publicly published and requires a custom sales quote
Does not include built-in payment processing or invoicing as a core feature
Value is strongest where both a restaurant's suppliers and the restaurant itself are on Choco
Primarily focused on food and beverage distribution, not a general-purpose B2B ordering tool

faqs.question: Is Choco free for restaurants?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Choco app is free for restaurants to order from their suppliers, with no listed fees.

faqs.question: How much does Choco cost for suppliers?
faqs.answer: Choco uses custom pricing for suppliers and distributors based on usage and features, with third-party estimates suggesting roughly $20-$25 per customer account per month; exact pricing requires a sales quote.

faqs.question: Does Choco process payments?
faqs.answer: Choco's core platform is focused on order and catalog management rather than payment processing or invoicing.

faqs.question: Where is Choco headquartered?
faqs.answer: Choco is headquartered in Berlin, Germany, and was founded there in 2018.

faqs.question: Does Choco have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Choco offers apps for both restaurants (iOS and Android) and a separate Sales Rep app for distributor field sales teams.
